comments.author: @Ginoto
comments.text: Nice video, but the brua part it’s not true. Of course it’s part of the identity and history, but it is not a big or important part of the most people.

comments.author: @dtmfnow
comments.text: As a local I did not like the introduction. Because of the climate and soil of the island, the Spanish Valencian Orange became the Laraja which is the key ingredient of the Famous Blue Curaçao. Learn from the Youtube channel KOMPAS a well educated Curaçaoan. Absence of father could be a lot of reasons, early dead. Check how many Shell workers after receiving a vaccine shortly when their early retirement began had just died.

replies.author: @LifeNest-3
replies.text: Thank you for sharing your perspective and providing more context about the local history.

comments.author: @augiewinter1348
comments.text: What generalist BS. Most people do not practice any "brua" and I can go on about every nonsense statement made. Modern families share the responsibility of the children and work and seu (not "seoul") is not the grandest festival, but Carnival which takes place in jan-feb-march. And tambu is not played during Carnival but tumba. Please don't follow this nonsense and I would never subscribe to this nonsense channel.

replies.author: @Rich-c2q2q
replies.text: This Video is full off shit missinformation and pictures from other places

comments.author: @kaykay7461
comments.text: AI. Please inform people you used AI generator to make this video misinforming them! 😂

comments.author: @SpecTre-to1uy
comments.text:
As a local, born and raised in Curaçao, I can definitely say that the “brua” practice you mentioned is NOT part of all families on the island. 

There is a small amount of people that believe in it and practice it, but definitely NOT something that all families practice as you mentioned.

As additional information, “Tambú”, “Tumba” and “Seú” are three different rhythms.

replies.author: @kabritu.
replies.text: lol AI a traha e video e ta fake

replies.author: @curamario
replies.text: As a local studying our culture, I can agree that Brua is not openly practiced in most families. However, it is astonishing how, in times of despair, illness, and financial need, people of all layers of society seek the help of either a kurandero healer, seer, or montado brua practitioner for a solution... And that could very well be a majority....

comments.author: @GlobalCountryExplorer
comments.text: Paradise is never the whole story. I enjoy documentaries like this because they remind me that every beautiful place has a history, challenges, and people whose stories are worth hearing.
